nativeRange.habitat: Humid lowland and montane tropical forest understory in the Indo-Malesian region; grows on moist forest floor and shaded banks in high-humidity microclimates

decorPreferences: moist, well-draining substrate (e.g. ABG mix, sphagnum, coco fibre)
NOT planted submerged in an aquarium — will rot and die underwater

goodWith: dart frog and tropical-vivarium plant communities
other high-humidity ferns and mosses (e.g. Pilea, Fittonia, Selaginella kraussiana)
avoidWith: fully submerged aquarium use (plant will die)
direct intense lighting (bleaches the iridescent layer)

summary: Iridescent blue-green spike moss from Southeast Asia. Important husbandry caveat: this is NOT a true aquatic plant — it is generally regarded as a paludarium, vivarium, terrarium, or wabi-kusa plant and tends to die if planted fully submerged.
careNotes:
TAXON-FIT WARNING (flagged for reviewer): Selaginella willdenowii is not a true aquatic plant and is widely documented as unable to survive prolonged full submersion. The Buce Plant product page (the assigned primary source) states directly: 'This is not an aquarium plant. Submerging it completely will result in the death of this plant.' Multiple secondary trade and hobby sources (AquascapeRoom, The Aquarium Guide, Barr Report Forum, fishywiki) corroborate that it is intended for paludariums, terrariums, wabi-kusa, and high-humidity vivariums — typically used emergent above the waterline or in spray-zone arrangements, not planted in the substrate of a standard aquarium. It was probably surfaced into the freshwater plant manifest because Buce Plant catalogues it under 'aquarium plants' alongside genuinely submersible species; the manifest entry therefore reflects a category-fit error in the upstream trade catalogue rather than a real aquatic plant. Recommendation to the reviewer: either re-categorize this entry as a paludarium / terrarium / vivarium plant (a category the current schema does not separately model), or remove it from the freshwater-plant slice and flag the discovery pipeline to exclude terrestrial Selaginella from Buce Plant scrapes going forward.

DISAMBIGUATION: the trade name 'Rainbow Plant' is also used for the genuinely-aquatic Echinodorus 'Rainbow' cultivar (a captive Amazon sword variant with pink/green variegated leaves). These two plants are completely unrelated taxonomically — the Echinodorus 'Rainbow' is a monocot flowering aquatic, while Selaginella willdenowii is a lycophyte spikemoss. If a user searches for 'Rainbow Plant' expecting a submerged-aquarium plant, they almost certainly want the Echinodorus cultivar, not this species. The more authoritative common names for this species are 'Peacock Fern' and 'Willdenow's Spikemoss'.

IDENTIFICATION AND APPEARANCE: Selaginella willdenowii is a vining/scrambling spikemoss in the family Selaginellaceae (lycophytes, NOT true ferns despite the 'peacock fern' trade name). Its mature foliage exhibits a striking iridescent blue-green sheen produced not by pigment but by thin-film interference from a lamellar cell layer in the upper leaf cuticle — the same optical mechanism as a peacock feather. The iridescence is strongest in shade and on healthy growth; bright direct light and stressed growth wash it out. Plants in the trade are typically sold as 12-24 inch (~30-60 cm) clumps that can scramble considerably wider given space and humidity.

NATIVE RANGE AND HABITAT: native to humid tropical Southeast Asia, from southern China and Bangladesh through Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, Peninsular Malaysia, Indonesia (Sumatra, Java, Sulawesi, and the rest of Malesia), and the Philippines. Grows on shaded forest floors, moist banks, and seepage zones in lowland and montane rainforest. The aquarium-tolerance literature is essentially non-existent because the species is not aquatic; the parameter ranges provided here describe the emergent / paludarium conditions the plant actually wants (around 18-25 °C, partial shade, indirect or low-to-medium light, slightly acidic to neutral substrate moisture, very high ambient humidity).

CULTIVATION (paludarium / vivarium / wabi-kusa): plant in well-draining moisture-retentive substrate (ABG mix, long-fibre sphagnum, coco fibre, or commercial vivarium soil); remove any cotton or rockwool wrapping the nursery sold it with. Keep humidity above ~70% — under a closed glass top, in a misted vivarium, or in a wabi-kusa ball. Indirect / shaded lighting suits it best; full sun bleaches the iridescent layer and crisps foliage. Yellowing leaves typically indicate over-watering or stagnant waterlogging rather than too little water. Tolerates regular misting and even brief flooding of the root zone but should not be planted with the crown underwater long-term. Growth rate is slow to moderate; trim trailing stems to control shape.

PROPAGATION: vegetative by stem cuttings or by layering trailing runners onto fresh substrate (each rooted node will form a new plant). True sexual reproduction (heterosporous, producing megaspores and microspores from strobili in a leaf-axil cone) is documented in the genus but is rarely attempted in cultivation.

CROSS-SOURCE NOTE: cross-verification produced highly consistent qualitative agreement across primary (Buce Plant) and secondary (AquascapeRoom, plus additional hobby sources surfaced by web search) on the central fact that this species is unsuitable for fully submerged aquarium use. Numeric parameter ranges (temperature, light, humidity) are not in real disagreement because most sources simply do not quote aquarium parameters at all — the parameter block above is therefore best-effort emergent/paludarium guidance, not aquarium water-column targets, and dataStatus is set to needs_review accordingly.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation by stem cuttings or layered runners is reliable in vivarium / paludarium setups; nodes in contact with moist substrate root readily. Heterosporous sexual reproduction (strobili producing micro- and megaspores) is essentially never observed under aquarium-hobby conditions.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Promoted to primary after the assigned Tropica search URL (https://tropica.com/en/search?q=Selaginella+willdenowii) returned only navigation/template content with no product result — Tropica does not appear to list this species, consistent with it not being an aquatic plant. Buce Plant product page provided care level (easy), light (low to medium), growth rate (slow to moderate), max size (12-24 inches / ~30-60 cm), origin label ('Africa' on their page, which contradicts authoritative taxonomic sources — Plants of the World Online, GBIF, and multiple botanical references all give a Southeast Asian / Indo-Malesian native range; the Buce Plant 'Africa' label is treated as a retailer error and overridden by the taxonomic consensus), and — critically — the explicit statement: 'This is not an aquarium plant. Submerging it completely will result in the death of this plant.' Family label on the Buce Plant page (Lycopodiaceae) is also incorrect — current taxonomy places Selaginella in Selaginellaceae, which is the family recorded above.

additional.name: AquascapeRoom — Selaginella Willdenowii for Terrariums and Paludariums
additional.notes: Cross-verified the paludarium/terrarium-only use case ('Selaginella Willdenowii is not suitable for full submersion'), the lighting preference ('moderate to low indirect light; avoid direct sunlight'), and the temperature range (18-25 °C / 65-77 °F). Confirms intended use is 'terrariums, paludariums, or humid indoor gardens.'

additional.name: Plants of the World Online (Kew) — Selaginella willdenowii
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority used to correct two errors on the Buce Plant page: (1) family is Selaginellaceae (not Lycopodiaceae), order Selaginellales, class Lycopodiopsida; (2) native range is Indo-Malesian Southeast Asia (Bangladesh through Indonesia and the Philippines), not Africa.

additional.name: The Aquarium Guide — Peacock Fern
additional.notes: Additional hobby-source corroboration of the genus-wide statement that no Selaginella species is truly aquatic, though some tolerate brief submersion. Supports the dataStatus = needs_review taxon-fit flag.

additional.name: Tropica search (assigned primary, no result)
additional.notes: Originally-assigned primary source. The search page returned no product hit for Selaginella willdenowii — consistent with Tropica curating only genuine aquatic plants and excluding terrestrial Selaginella from their catalogue. Documented for traceability; the primary role was reassigned to Buce Plant per the playbook's primary-failure fallback.

propagation: layering trailing runners onto moist substrate
